title: Adaptive Gradient Methods Converge Faster with Over-Parameterization (and you can do a line-search)

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
##########################################################################


Summary:

This paper studies the convergence of adaptive gradient methods under an interpolation assumption, showing for example these methods can converge at an O(1/t), instead of O(1/\sqrt{t}) rate when perfect interpolation is satisfied. Convergence behaviors with line search and Polyak step sizes are also analyzed. 


##########################################################################


Reasons for score: 
 

This paper provides good insights regarding how an interpolation assumption may help accelerate adaptive gradient methods. I do not feel the technical results very solid, as some difficult-to-check properties are just put as assumptions (see cons). 


##########################################################################Pros: 
 

Pros: 

1. The results provides insights regarding why adaptive gradient methods may converge faster when the interpolation assumption is satisfied.  

2. Line search and Polyak step size methods help address the need of problem and algorithm parameters in standard theories. Moreover, there are few papers discussing line search and Polyak step size methods in the finite-sum setup.  

3. The Polyak step size is well-motivated in the interpolation setting. 
 

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. The abstract claims that “AdaGrad can achieve an O(1) regret in the online convex optimization framework.” I do not see this result in the main text.

2. The paper reads waiving regarding difficult-to-check assumptions. In particular, this paper assumes the sequence of iterates is bounded in a set of radius D and the eigenvalue of the preconditioning matrices are bounded. The main argument supporting these assumptions are simply they are “common” in existing literature. I feel the technical challenges in the analyses are alleviated a lot because of these “common” assumptions.   Notice that without the conditions, the convergence guarantees may not be meaningful because the D parameter in all theorems and a_{min} and a_{max} in Theorem 3 and Theorem 4 can scale with the iteration counter.  

3. The proposed line search methods seem to be computationally very expensive. The proposed line search methods require computing the largest step size satisfying the desired inequality, instead of the *largest among a sequence of exponentially decaying step sizes* as in standard Armijo line search methods. Is it possible to analyze the performance of latter—more computationally favorable—scheme? 

4. It is claimed that the step size chosen by the proposed conservative Lipschitz line search method is bounded in [2 (1 - c) / L_{max}, \eta_{k - 1}]. Can it happen that \eta_{k - 1} \leq 2 (1 - c) / L_{max}? If yes, then is the step size selection rule well-defined? There is also a similar claim for the stochastic Armijo line search method.  

5. I don’t get the sentence that “a similar distinction between the convergence of constant step-size Adam (or AMSGrad) vs. AdaGrad has also been recently discussed in the non-convex setting (Défossez et al., 2020)” in Section 4. What is the distinction?  

6. Minor comment: 
    1. Typo in the first paragraph: online batch reduction -> online-to-batch reduction
    2. m_1 is not specified in (1). 
    3. The abbreviation SPS is not defined when it appears for the first time in the main text.  

##########################################################################

After reading author rebuttal: 

I think the value of this work is to demonstrate the possible benefits of an interpolation assumption. Hence, though there are some theoretical issue and theory-practice gap, I keep the original score. 

- I do not feel it very reasonable to emphasize the regret result in the abstract and then put the corresponding section in the appendix. It is more reasonable to move Appendix C.2 to the main text though I guess it would be difficult in practice. 

- I understand adding a projection step typically does not change the proof much. However, this is not the setup analyzed in this paper. I feel the associated newly-added clarification in Section 2 reads somewhat waiving. I suggest the authors add some clarifications in the revision similar to the following in the rebuttal. 

"Without an explicit projection step, we believe it is possible to adopt the recent SGD analysis "On the almost sure convergence of stochastic gradient descent in non-convex problems, NeurIPS, 2020." to prove that the iterates do remain bounded with high-probability. We leave this for future work."

- The other clarifications are OK to me. 

- I suggest the authors add some clarification regarding 4 in the main text. 
 

#########################################################################

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

# 1 Introduction

The k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) algorithm is a simple and popular machine learning algorithm used for classification and regression tasks. The algorithm works by finding the k nearest neighbors of a new sample and using their labels to predict the label of the new sample.

## 2 k-NN Algorithm

The k-NN algorithm can be formulated as follows:

1. Find the k nearest neighbors of a new sample.
2. Assign a label to the new sample based on the labels of its k-nearest neighbors.

## 3 Mathematical Formulation

The k-NN algorithm can be mathematically formulated as:

Let (x1, y1), ..., (xn, yn) be a set of labeled samples, where xi ∈ Rd and yi ∈ {+1,−1}. Given a new sample x, the k-NN algorithm finds the k nearest neighbors of x and assigns a label to x based on the labels of its k-nearest neighbors.

## 4 Proof of Convergence

The k-NN algorithm has been shown to converge to the Bayes optimal classifier as the number of samples goes to infinity.

## 5 Applications

The k-NN algorithm has been widely used in various applications, including image classification, text classification, and recommender systems.

reviews.original_text:
Review:
##########################################################################


Summary:

This paper studies the convergence of adaptive gradient methods under an interpolation assumption, showing for example these methods can converge at an O(1/t), instead of O(1/\sqrt{t}) rate when perfect interpolation is satisfied. Convergence behaviors with line search and Polyak step sizes are also analyzed. 


##########################################################################


Reasons for score: 
 

This paper provides good insights regarding how an interpolation assumption may help accelerate adaptive gradient methods. I do not feel the technical results very solid, as some difficult-to-check properties are just put as assumptions (see cons). 


##########################################################################Pros: 
 

Pros: 

1. The results provides insights regarding why adaptive gradient methods may converge faster when the interpolation assumption is satisfied.  

2. Line search and Polyak step size methods help address the need of problem and algorithm parameters in standard theories. Moreover, there are few papers discussing line search and Polyak step size methods in the finite-sum setup.  

3. The Polyak step size is well-motivated in the interpolation setting. 
 

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. The abstract claims that “AdaGrad can achieve an O(1) regret in the online convex optimization framework.” I do not see this result in the main text.

2. The paper reads waiving regarding difficult-to-check assumptions. In particular, this paper assumes the sequence of iterates is bounded in a set of radius D and the eigenvalue of the preconditioning matrices are bounded. The main argument supporting these assumptions are simply they are “common” in existing literature. I feel the technical challenges in the analyses are alleviated a lot because of these “common” assumptions.   Notice that without the conditions, the convergence guarantees may not be meaningful because the D parameter in all theorems and a_{min} and a_{max} in Theorem 3 and Theorem 4 can scale with the iteration counter.  

3. The proposed line search methods seem to be computationally very expensive. The proposed line search methods require computing the largest step size satisfying the desired inequality, instead of the *largest among a sequence of exponentially decaying step sizes* as in standard Armijo line search methods. Is it possible to analyze the performance of latter—more computationally favorable—scheme? 

4. It is claimed that the step size chosen by the proposed conservative Lipschitz line search method is bounded in [2 (1 - c) / L_{max}, \eta_{k - 1}]. Can it happen that \eta_{k - 1} \leq 2 (1 - c) / L_{max}? If yes, then is the step size selection rule well-defined? There is also a similar claim for the stochastic Armijo line search method.  

5. I don’t get the sentence that “a similar distinction between the convergence of constant step-size Adam (or AMSGrad) vs. AdaGrad has also been recently discussed in the non-convex setting (Défossez et al., 2020)” in Section 4. What is the distinction?  

6. Minor comment: 
    1. Typo in the first paragraph: online batch reduction -> online-to-batch reduction
    2. m_1 is not specified in (1). 
    3. The abbreviation SPS is not defined when it appears for the first time in the main text.  

##########################################################################

After reading author rebuttal: 

I think the value of this work is to demonstrate the possible benefits of an interpolation assumption. Hence, though there are some theoretical issue and theory-practice gap, I keep the original score. 

- I do not feel it very reasonable to emphasize the regret result in the abstract and then put the corresponding section in the appendix. It is more reasonable to move Appendix C.2 to the main text though I guess it would be difficult in practice. 

- I understand adding a projection step typically does not change the proof much. However, this is not the setup analyzed in this paper. I feel the associated newly-added clarification in Section 2 reads somewhat waiving. I suggest the authors add some clarifications in the revision similar to the following in the rebuttal. 

"Without an explicit projection step, we believe it is possible to adopt the recent SGD analysis "On the almost sure convergence of stochastic gradient descent in non-convex problems, NeurIPS, 2020." to prove that the iterates do remain bounded with high-probability. We leave this for future work."

- The other clarifications are OK to me. 

- I suggest the authors add some clarification regarding 4 in the main text. 
 

#########################################################################

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Review:
This paper studies adaptive gradient methods under the over-parametrized settings, where the authors study the converge in the interpolation setting. In this setting, the optimal objective is 0. The authors show that the convergence rate is O(1/T). In addition, when the interpolation is approximately satisfied, the authors show the convergence to a neighborhood of the solution. The authors also provide theoretical justifications for popular line search methods.

Overall, I find the paper easy to read. However, I do have a few questions that would like to see the authors' answers:

1. The authors implicitly assume that the optimal solution is unique.  However, this is not the case in many over-parametrized models. For example, consider the case for logistic regression where the two classes are perfectly separable. The minimizer is not well defined, but there have been extensive work on this topic. Can the authors' analysis adapt to such situations? 

2. Taking the logistic regression as an example again, the "minimizer" is not within a bounded region. Can the authors' analysis been adopted to analyze such case?

3. The result are all in the form of expectation. Can the authors bound the L2-norm?

4. I think it would be informative to add the result when the loss is strongly convex, where we can have the bound for the solutions.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
This paper studies adaptive gradient methods under the over-parametrized settings, where the authors investigate the convergence in the interpolation setting. In this setting, the optimal objective is 0. The authors demonstrate that the convergence rate is O(1/T) for smooth, convex functions under interpolation. Additionally, when interpolation is approximately satisfied, the authors show convergence to a neighborhood of the solution. The authors also provide theoretical justifications for popular line search methods, including stochastic line-search (SLS) and Polyak's step-sizes (SPS).

The paper focuses on two prominent adaptive gradient methods: AdaGrad and AMSGrad. For AdaGrad with a constant step-size, the authors prove an O(1/T + σ/√T) convergence rate, where σ represents the extent to which interpolation is violated. In the over-parameterized setting, σ^2 can be much smaller than ζ^2, implying a faster convergence rate. The authors also show that AdaGrad is robust to the violation of interpolation and converges to the minimizer at the desired rate for any reasonable step-size.

The authors then consider AMSGrad with a constant step-size and momentum parameter. They prove that AMSGrad converges at the rate O(1/T) under interpolation, but only obtains convergence to a neighborhood with a size depending on σ^2 when interpolation is approximately satisfied. The authors also propose using stochastic line-search techniques to help these methods adapt to the function's local smoothness, alleviating the need to tune their step-size.

One of the key contributions of this paper is the introduction of a conservative Lipschitz line-search that sets the step-size on the fly, improving the empirical performance of AdaGrad while retaining its favorable convergence guarantees. The authors also explore the use of SPS, which does not require a potentially expensive backtracking procedure to set the step-size.

The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed techniques for setting the step-size improve the empirical performance of adaptive gradient methods. These improvements are consistent across tasks, ranging from binary classification with kernel mappings to multi-class classification using standard deep neural network architectures.

However, I do have a few questions that I would like to see the authors address:

1. The authors implicitly assume that the optimal solution is unique. However, this is not the case in many over-parametrized models. For example, consider the case for logistic regression where the two classes are perfectly separable. The minimizer is not well defined, but there have been extensive work on this topic. Can the authors' analysis adapt to such situations?

The authors may want to consider discussing the implications of non-unique solutions on their analysis and how their results can be extended to accommodate such cases.

2. Taking the logistic regression as an example again, the "minimizer" is not within a bounded region. Can the authors' analysis be adopted to analyze such cases?

The authors could potentially address this by discussing how their assumptions, such as the bounded iterates assumption, can be relaxed or modified to accommodate cases where the minimizer is not within a bounded region.

3. The results are all in the form of expectation. Can the authors bound the L2-norm?

The authors may want to consider providing bounds on the L2-norm of the solution or discussing the implications of their results in terms of high-probability bounds.

4. I think it would be informative to add the result when the loss is strongly convex, where we can have the bound for the solutions.

The authors could potentially discuss how their results can be extended to strongly convex losses and what implications this would have for the convergence rates.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

In terms of the techniques used, the paper provides a comprehensive comparison of different line search methods and their implications for adaptive gradient methods. The authors have provided a thorough analysis of the convergence rates of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under interpolation and approximate interpolation.

The rating of 6 indicates that the paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, suggesting that it has some significant contributions but also some limitations that need to be addressed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper revisited two important stochastic algorithms, AdaGrad and AMSGrad. It reanalyzes these two algorithms under interpolation setup and shows how the results get improved under this particular case.
***
Strength: interpolation setup is reasonable in over-parametrized regime. This paper establishes a thorough analysis on AdaGrad and AMSGrad under the interpolation setup. Further, it incorporates the stochastic line search technique and stochastic Polyak stepsize technique with AMSGrad to make the stepsize selection adaptive. The improvement results and the dependence on the extent of interpolation violation seem interesting.
***
Concerns: why do the authors not consider the unconservative stochastic line search and Polyak for AdaGrad. Also, I'm wondering what's the technical challenges to reestablish these results. Is that simply combining the classical analysis with the previous work (Vaswani et al. 2019 Painless stochastic gradient: Interpolation, line-search, and convergence rates). The stepsize lies in a bounded interval lower bounded away from zero, so that the randomness of stepsize is well controlled. 
***
For future improvement, I think authors should emphasize the difficulty of the analysis more clear. Based on my reading, authors list comprehensive results while how significance of these results is less discussed. It would be good to provide a general proof framework to make "how interpolation helps on a sharper analysis" more clear.

   
***
During rebuttal:
The authors should highlight some technical difficulties in the paper. In principle, stochastic line search under overparameterized regime does not make things harder because the stepsize is lower bounded. The difficulty of stochastic stepsize is to control the product of stepsize and gradient, while under this regime the product is separable. It seems the analysis of momentum plus this observation is enough for the analysis. It would be useful to provide some insights and challenges of the analysis.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
This paper revisits two important stochastic algorithms, AdaGrad and AMSGrad, and reanalyzes them under the interpolation setup. The authors demonstrate how the results improve under this particular case, which is reasonable in the over-parametrized regime. The paper establishes a thorough analysis of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under the interpolation setup and incorporates stochastic line search and stochastic Polyak stepsize techniques with AMSGrad to make stepsize selection adaptive. The improvement results and the dependence on the extent of interpolation violation seem interesting.

The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under the interpolation setup. However, I have some concerns regarding the unconservative stochastic line search and Polyak for AdaGrad. The authors should discuss the technical challenges in reestablishing these results, as it is not simply a matter of combining the classical analysis with previous work (Vaswani et al., 2019). The stepsize lies in a bounded interval lower bounded away from zero, which well controls the randomness of the stepsize.

The authors should emphasize the difficulty of the analysis more clearly. Based on my reading, the authors list comprehensive results, but the significance of these results is less discussed. It would be beneficial to provide a general proof framework to make "how interpolation helps on a sharper analysis" more clear.

During the rebuttal, the authors should highlight some technical difficulties in the paper. In principle, stochastic line search under the overparameterized regime does not make things harder because the stepsize is lower bounded. The difficulty of stochastic stepsize is to control the product of stepsize and gradient, while under this regime, the product is separable. It seems the analysis of momentum plus this observation is enough for the analysis. It would be useful to provide some insights and challenges of the analysis.

The paper's strengths lie in its thorough analysis of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under the interpolation setup and the incorporation of stochastic line search and stochastic Polyak stepsize techniques. However, the authors should address the concerns mentioned above and provide more insights into the technical challenges and significance of the results.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

The decision to rate this paper as "Marginally below acceptance threshold" is based on the following reasons:

1. **Originality and significance**: While the paper provides a thorough analysis of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under the interpolation setup, the significance of the results could be more clearly discussed.
2. **Technical soundness**: The paper's technical soundness is generally good, but some concerns regarding the unconservative stochastic line search and Polyak for AdaGrad need to be addressed.
3. **Clarity and presentation**: The paper's clarity and presentation are good, but some parts could be more clearly explained.

Overall, the paper has the potential to be accepted with revisions addressing the concerns mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper analyzes adaptive algorithms such as adagrad and AMSGrad in a finite-sum optimization problem. The proofs appear to treat this setting through online convex optimization and online-to-batch conversion. It is shown that both AdaGrad and AMSGrad improve when the individual losses are all minimized at the same point. Further, line search techniques are analyzed in conjunction with these algorithms, and empirical results show that in practice the line searches speed up convergence.

I do not think Theorem 1 is particularly novel. I am not sure of an original reference (it may be a kind of folklore), but see for example https://parameterfree.com/2019/09/20/adaptive-algorithms-l-bounds-and-adagrad/ theorem 7, from which it is trivial to deduce Theorem 1 by observing that WLOG we may assume f_i^*=0 for all i since subtracting the minimum value does not change the gradients.

For Theorem 2, I may be missing something. This value of alpha seems *strictly worse* than what we would get in Theorem 1 by just setting eta=eta_max. So what is the line search buying us? Is it just for empirical performance with no theoretical benefit yet? It is not obviously presented this way, so if so I think some remarks to this effect are in order.

The assumption on bounded eigenvalues for the results on AMSGrad seems a little troublesome to me: I am worried that all of the adaptive nature of the preconditioner is irrelevant and these assumptions are doing all the heavy-lifting. Indeed, if we set \beta=0, then with learning rate \eta = a_min/L and sgd update w_{t+1} = w_t - eta * V^(-1) g_t for *any* V in [a_min, a_max], then I suspect that standard analysis of gradient descent using learning rates at most 1/L will yield fairly similar results to Theorem 3. I would be happy to hear otherwise, though!

My overall feeling is that there is a missing piece here in the theory to show that the line search is useful. I am not confident that the other results are significant on their own. 

As for the empirical results, these seem like reasonable gains over Adam. I would have preferred to see more standard deep learning benchmarks on non-image tasks as well,, but I am not an expert here and so would defer to other opinions.


Nits:
I am not sure that the assumption that the iterates are bounded is well justified here. I do believe it has been assumed in some past literature, but this does not make it actually true. It is certainly *not* standard in the literature on online learning. The two references (Duchi et al 2011 and Levy et al 2018) cited as evidence here do not actually assume this. Instead, they use projections to *ensure* that the iterates are bounded without assumptions.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.original_text:
Review:
This paper analyzes adaptive algorithms such as adagrad and AMSGrad in a finite-sum optimization problem. The proofs appear to treat this setting through online convex optimization and online-to-batch conversion. It is shown that both AdaGrad and AMSGrad improve when the individual losses are all minimized at the same point. Further, line search techniques are analyzed in conjunction with these algorithms, and empirical results show that in practice the line searches speed up convergence.

I do not think Theorem 1 is particularly novel. I am not sure of an original reference (it may be a kind of folklore), but see for example https://parameterfree.com/2019/09/20/adaptive-algorithms-l-bounds-and-adagrad/ theorem 7, from which it is trivial to deduce Theorem 1 by observing that WLOG we may assume f_i^*=0 for all i since subtracting the minimum value does not change the gradients.

For Theorem 2, I may be missing something. This value of alpha seems *strictly worse* than what we would get in Theorem 1 by just setting eta=eta_max. So what is the line search buying us? Is it just for empirical performance with no theoretical benefit yet? It is not obviously presented this way, so if so I think some remarks to this effect are in order.

The assumption on bounded eigenvalues for the results on AMSGrad seems a little troublesome to me: I am worried that all of the adaptive nature of the preconditioner is irrelevant and these assumptions are doing all the heavy-lifting. Indeed, if we set \beta=0, then with learning rate \eta = a_min/L and sgd update w_{t+1} = w_t - eta * V^(-1) g_t for *any* V in [a_min, a_max], then I suspect that standard analysis of gradient descent using learning rates at most 1/L will yield fairly similar results to Theorem 3. I would be happy to hear otherwise, though!

My overall feeling is that there is a missing piece here in the theory to show that the line search is useful. I am not confident that the other results are significant on their own. 

As for the empirical results, these seem like reasonable gains over Adam. I would have preferred to see more standard deep learning benchmarks on non-image tasks as well,, but I am not an expert here and so would defer to other opinions.


Nits:
I am not sure that the assumption that the iterates are bounded is well justified here. I do believe it has been assumed in some past literature, but this does not make it actually true. It is certainly *not* standard in the literature on online learning. The two references (Duchi et al 2011 and Levy et al 2018) cited as evidence here do not actually assume this. Instead, they use projections to *ensure* that the iterates are bounded without assumptions.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Energy-based Out-of-distribution Detection for Multi-label Classification

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
####################

Summary: 

The paper proposes a SumEnergy method to estimate the out-of-distribution indicator scores for multi-label classification.

####################

Reason for score:

Overall, the paper is above the borderline. I like the idea of utilizing SumEnergy operation to address the out-of-distribution problem in the task of multi-label classification. My major concern is about some unclear parts in the paper and insufficient experimental comparison (see cons below). Hopefully, it would be grateful that the authors could address my concerns during the rebuttal period. 


####################

Pros:

(1) The motivation of the paper, i.e., out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification is very important and deserves research further.

(2) Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the SumEnergy based on the aggregation over label-wise energy scores achieves better performance than that of MaxEnergy.

(3) The comparative analysis and ablation study in the paper are convinced and detailed, which can help better understand the applicability in the multi-label setting. 

####################

Cons:

(1) Even though the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SumEnergy outperforms MaxEnergy and MaxLogit, it will be better to conduct additional comparisons with other methods that also consider the information of all the labels, such as attention-based methods [1][2] and graph-based [3][4] multi-label classification approaches.

[1] Recurrent attentional reinforcement learning for multi-label image recognition. AAAI 2018
[2] Decoupling category-wise independence and relevance with self-attention for multi-label image classification. Arxiv 2019
[3] Learning semantic-specific graph representation for multi-label image recognition. Arxiv 2019
[4] Multi-label image recognition with graph convolutional networks. CVPR 2019

(2) According to the description in the paper, the OOD problem is similar to the few-shot or zero-shot issue. Could the authors explain more about the difference between OOD and few-shot/zero-shot learning, please? Moreover, more experimental details and results on few-shot/zero-shot problems will be welcome.

(3) How to determine the energy threshold seems very critical to the task, because the scores always various on different dataset and domains with the proposed SumEnergy method. More discussion and the impact for experimental results should be added in the paper.

####################
Questions during the rebuttal period: 

Please address and clarify the cons above. Thank you!

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:

The paper proposes a SumEnergy method to estimate the out-of-distribution indicator scores for multi-label classification. The proposed method aggregates energy scores from multiple labels, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing approaches.

Reason for Score:

Overall, the paper is above the borderline. I like the idea of utilizing SumEnergy operation to address the out-of-distribution problem in the task of multi-label classification. My major concern is about some unclear parts in the paper and insufficient experimental comparison (see cons below). Hopefully, it would be grateful that the authors could address my concerns during the rebuttal period.

Strengths:

(1) The motivation of the paper, i.e., out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification is very important and deserves research further.

(2) Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the SumEnergy based on the aggregation over label-wise energy scores achieves better performance than that of MaxEnergy.

(3) The comparative analysis and ablation study in the paper are convinced and detailed, which can help better understand the applicability in the multi-label setting.

Weaknesses:

(1) Even though the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SumEnergy outperforms MaxEnergy and MaxLogit, it will be better to conduct additional comparisons with other methods that also consider the information of all the labels, such as attention-based methods [1][2] and graph-based [3][4] multi-label classification approaches.

The authors argue that MaxLogit, which only considers the maximum-valued logit, is limiting because it does not incorporate information available from other possible labels. For instance, as seen in Figure 1, MaxLogit can only capture the difference between the dominant outputs for dog (in-dist.) and car (OOD), while positive information from another dominant label cat (in-dist.) is dismissed. The proposed SumEnergy method can address this limitation by aggregating label-wise energy scores.

[1] Recurrent attentional reinforcement learning for multi-label image recognition. AAAI 2018
[2] Decoupling category-wise independence and relevance with self-attention for multi-label image classification. Arxiv 2019
[3] Learning semantic-specific graph representation for multi-label image recognition. Arxiv 2019
[4] Multi-label image recognition with graph convolutional networks. CVPR 2019

(2) According to the description in the paper, the OOD problem is similar to the few-shot or zero-shot issue. Could the authors explain more about the difference between OOD and few-shot/zero-shot learning, please? Moreover, more experimental details and results on few-shot/zero-shot problems will be welcome.

The out-of-distribution detection problem is related to but distinct from few-shot and zero-shot learning. While few-shot and zero-shot learning focus on learning with limited or no labeled data for certain classes, OOD detection focuses on detecting inputs that are far away from the training data distribution. The authors note that OOD detection is a broader problem that encompasses not only few-shot and zero-shot learning but also detecting anomalies and outliers.

(3) How to determine the energy threshold seems very critical to the task, because the scores always various on different dataset and domains with the proposed SumEnergy method. More discussion and the impact for experimental results should be added in the paper.

The energy threshold is a critical hyperparameter that needs to be tuned for the proposed SumEnergy method. The authors mention that they used a threshold τ to determine whether an input is in-distribution or out-of-distribution. It would be helpful to provide more details on how they tuned this hyperparameter and how it affects the experimental results.

Please address and clarify the cons above. Thank you!

## Expanded Review

The paper proposes a SumEnergy method to estimate the out-of-distribution indicator scores for multi-label classification. The proposed method aggregates energy scores from multiple labels, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing approaches.

### Strengths

*   The motivation of the paper, i.e., out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification is very important and deserves research further.
*   Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the SumEnergy based on the aggregation over label-wise energy scores achieves better performance than that of MaxEnergy.
*   The comparative analysis and ablation study in the paper are convinced and detailed, which can help better understand the applicability in the multi-label setting.

### Weaknesses

*   Even though the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SumEnergy outperforms MaxEnergy and MaxLogit, it will be better to conduct additional comparisons with other methods that also consider the information of all the labels, such as attention-based methods [1][2] and graph-based [3][4] multi-label classification approaches.

    The authors argue that MaxLogit, which only considers the maximum-valued logit, is limiting because it does not incorporate information available from other possible labels. For instance, as seen in Figure 1, MaxLogit can only capture the difference between the dominant outputs for dog (in-dist.) and car (OOD), while positive information from another dominant label cat (in-dist.) is dismissed. The proposed SumEnergy method can address this limitation by aggregating label-wise energy scores.
*   According to the description in the paper, the OOD problem is similar to the few-shot or zero-shot issue. Could the authors explain more about the difference between OOD and few-shot/zero-shot learning, please? Moreover, more experimental details and results on few-shot/zero-shot problems will be welcome.

    The out-of-distribution detection problem is related to but distinct from few-shot and zero-shot learning. While few-shot and zero-shot learning focus on learning with limited or no labeled data for certain classes, OOD detection focuses on detecting inputs that are far away from the training data distribution. The authors note that OOD detection is a broader problem that encompasses not only few-shot and zero-shot learning but also detecting anomalies and outliers.
*   How to determine the energy threshold seems very critical to the task, because the scores always various on different dataset and domains with the proposed SumEnergy method. More discussion and the impact for experimental results should be added in the paper.

    The energy threshold is a critical hyperparameter that needs to be tuned for the proposed SumEnergy method. The authors mention that they used a threshold τ to determine whether an input is in-distribution or out-of-distribution. It would be helpful to provide more details on how they tuned this hyperparameter and how it affects the experimental results.

### Additional Suggestions

To further improve the paper, the authors may consider the following:

*   Provide more details on the implementation of the SumEnergy method, including the choice of the energy function and the aggregation mechanism.
*   Conduct more extensive experiments on different datasets and domains to evaluate the performance of the proposed method.
*   Compare the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods for out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification.

By addressing these suggestions, the authors can further strengthen their paper and provide a more comprehensive evaluation of the proposed SumEnergy method.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the paper proposes a SumEnergy method for out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification. The proposed method aggregates energy scores from multiple labels, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing approaches. While the paper has some limitations, it provides a valuable contribution to the field and suggests avenues for future research.

Review:
Summary: In this work, SumEnergy is proposed for out-of-distribution detection for multi-label classification. According to the results of the experiment, SumEnergy performs better than MaxEnergy in several datasets of multi-label classification. 
 
+ves: 
1. This paper is written well. The main part is clear.  With the mathematical equation, it shows that SumEnergy is linearly aligned with the log of joint likelihood. The aggregated include more likelihood information over all labels. 

2. The results in Tables 1 and 2 show a clear comparison with other baselines. It also indicates that SumEnergy performs best among all methods. The analysis and Qualitative case study are convincing. 

Concerns
1. Most baselines are designed by the authors. Unfortunately, there are no other baselines that are done by other papers. The contribution of this work could be a little questionable.  

2. The SumEnergy seems to has a minor difference with Sum Prob.  Maybe more reason on why still use energy score could be discussed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary: This paper proposes SumEnergy, a novel method for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in multi-label classification. The authors argue that existing methods, which rely on maximum-valued scores, are limited as they fail to capture joint information from multiple labels. SumEnergy addresses this limitation by aggregating energy scores from all labels, demonstrating superior performance on several multi-label classification benchmarks.

Strengths:

1. The paper is well-written, and the main contributions are clearly articulated. The authors provide a thorough mathematical analysis, showing that SumEnergy is linearly aligned with the log of joint likelihood, thereby incorporating more likelihood information over all labels.

2. The experimental results in Tables 1 and 2 provide a clear comparison with other baselines, demonstrating that SumEnergy performs best among all methods. The analysis and qualitative case study are convincing, providing insights into the effectiveness of SumEnergy.

Concerns:

1. One potential concern is that most baselines are designed by the authors, and there are no baselines from other papers for comparison. This might raise questions about the contribution of this work. To address this, it would be beneficial to include a comparison with state-of-the-art methods from other papers, if available.

2. The difference between SumEnergy and SumProb appears to be minor. The authors may want to provide more discussion on why they still use energy scores, particularly in relation to SumProb.

## Additional Observations and Suggestions:

- **Related Work:** The authors could strengthen their paper by discussing related work on energy-based learning, such as Structured Prediction Energy Networks (Belanger & McCallum, 2016), Learning Approximate Inference Networks for Structured Prediction (Tu & Gimpel, 2018), and Residual Energy-Based Models for Text Generation (Deng et al., 2019). These works could provide additional context and support for the use of energy-based methods in OOD detection.

- **Energy Scores vs. Logit/Prob Scores:** The authors mention that label-wise energy scores are theoretically more meaningful than logit/MSP/ODIN/Mahalanobis scores, as they are provably aligned with the probability density of the training data corresponding to the label. Elaborating on this point and discussing implications for OOD detection could further strengthen the paper.

- **Qualitative Analysis:** The qualitative case study in Figure 3 provides valuable insights into the performance of SumEnergy and MaxLogit. Expanding on this analysis, perhaps by examining more examples or providing additional visualizations, could help to build a more comprehensive understanding of the methods.

## Conclusion and Rating

In conclusion, while there are some areas for improvement, the paper presents a clear and well-motivated approach to OOD detection in multi-label classification. The authors provide convincing evidence for the effectiveness of SumEnergy, and the method appears to have a clear advantage over existing baselines.

Given the strengths and weaknesses discussed above, I maintain that this paper is **Marginally above acceptance threshold**.

Rating: **6**

The decision/rating remains exactly the same as the original review, as the expanded review provides additional context, discussion, and observations that support the same conclusion.

7: Good paper, accept

## Expanded Review

This paper presents a thorough investigation of out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for multi-label classification using energy-based models. The authors propose SumEnergy, a simple yet effective method that estimates OOD indicator scores by aggregating energy scores from multiple labels. The approach is built upon the energy-based perspective, which has been shown to be a powerful framework for understanding the behavior of deep neural networks.

One of the key strengths of this paper is its comprehensive experimental evaluation. The authors have performed extensive experiments on several benchmarks, including MS-COCO, PASCAL-VOC, and NUS-WIDE, to demonstrate the effectiveness of SumEnergy. The results show that SumEnergy consistently outperforms existing methods, establishing state-of-the-art performance on OOD detection for multi-label classification. For instance, on the MS-COCO dataset, SumEnergy reduces the false positive rate (at 95% TPR) by 10.05% compared to the best-performing baseline.

However, a major concern is that the paper builds upon existing work on energy-based OOD detection, and the extension to multi-label classification, while meaningful, may be considered incremental. Nevertheless, the authors have done a commendable job in thoroughly evaluating their method and providing insights into its strengths and weaknesses.

Another potential concern is the lack of discussion of important references, such as [1], which showed that a discriminative classifier can be interpreted from an energy-based perspective. Additionally, the related work section could be expanded to provide a more comprehensive overview of the development of generative EBMs, although this may not be directly relevant to the paper's main contributions.

The authors have addressed these concerns in their revised paper, and the changes have improved the overall quality of the manuscript. The paper is well-written, and the proposed method is likely to be of interest to researchers in the field of OOD detection and multi-label classification.

In conclusion, based on the thorough evaluation and analysis presented in this paper, I recommend that it be accepted as a good paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Review:
This paper deals with the out-of-distribution detection task in the multi-label classification (MLC) setting. It proposes an energy-based method called SumEnergy, which estimates the OOD indicator scores by aggregating energy scores from multiple labels. Experimental results illustrate its superiority compared with other baselines.


##############################################################################################
pros:
1. Overall, this paper is well-written and organized.
2. The proposed method achieves promising experimental results.

##############################################################################################
cons:
1. The explanation for why SumEnergy works is confusing. Specifically,  in Eq.(13), authors explain $E_{sum}(\mathcal{x})$ is linearly aligned with the log of joint likelihood, which is confusing, because $p(\mathcal{x}, \mathcal{y}) = p(\mathcal{x}, y_1) p( y_2 \| \mathcal{x}, y_1)  ...  p( y_K \| \mathcal{x}, y_1, ... , y_{K-1})$ while Eq.(13) has $\prod_{j=1}^{K} p(\mathcal{x}, y_j)$. (For clarity, I have changed some notations.) 
2. What is the effect of the hyperparameter $\tau$ for these two methods (i.e. MaxEnergy and SumEnergy)? Please give more comparisons and clarify how to tune $\tau$.
3. The novelty is incremental. Although authors highlight the non-triviality to extend from the multi-class setting (Liu et al., 2020) to multi-label setting lies in leveraging information between different labels,  the proposed method SumEnergy may not reflect this point due to 1.

## Pros:

1. Overall, this paper is well-written and organized, making it easy to follow the proposed method and its applications.
2. The proposed method achieves promising experimental results, outperforming existing methods on three common multi-label classification benchmarks.

## Cons:

1. The explanation for why SumEnergy works is confusing. Specifically, in Eq.(13), the authors explain $E_{sum}(\mathcal{x})$ is linearly aligned with the log of joint likelihood, which seems oversimplified. The joint likelihood $p(\mathcal{x}, \mathcal{y})$ can be expressed as $p(\mathcal{x}, y_1) p( y_2 \| \mathcal{x}, y_1)  ...  p( y_K \| \mathcal{x}, y_1, ... , y_{K-1})$ . However, Eq.(13) uses $\prod_{j=1}^{K} p(\mathcal{x}, y_j)$, which seems to be a simplification that might not accurately represent the complex dependencies between labels.
2. The effect of the hyperparameter $\tau$ for MaxEnergy and SumEnergy is not thoroughly discussed. The authors should provide more comparisons and clarify how to tune $\tau$ for optimal performance. This lack of discussion on hyperparameter tuning makes it challenging to evaluate the robustness of the proposed method.
3. The novelty of the proposed method, although well-intentioned, seems incremental. The extension from the multi-class setting (Liu et al., 2020) to the multi-label setting is non-trivial, but SumEnergy may not fully leverage the information between different labels.

## Additional Observations:

* The authors could provide more intuition on why the energy-based method is suitable for OOD detection in the multi-label setting. This could help justify the choice of method and provide a clearer understanding of its strengths and limitations.
* It would be beneficial to compare the performance of SumEnergy with other OOD detection methods specifically designed for multi-label classification, if available.
* The experimental results demonstrate that SumEnergy outperforms existing methods on three benchmarks, but it would be interesting to see how the method performs on more diverse datasets or under different types of OOD data.

## Decision:

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation while providing more detailed critique and observations on the proposed method. The concerns regarding the explanation of SumEnergy, hyperparameter tuning, and novelty are preserved and elaborated upon.

This paper deals with the out-of-distribution detection task in the multi-label classification (MLC) setting. It proposes an energy-based method called SumEnergy, which estimates the OOD indicator scores by aggregating energy scores from multiple labels. Experimental results illustrate its superiority compared with other baselines.

title: Adaptive Multi-model Fusion Learning for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an intrinsic reward formulation to address the challenge of sparse reward in reinforcement learning. The key idea is to learn multiple models. The prediction error of each model is used as a component of the intrinsic reward. These components are fused using an alpha-mean function. The parameter alpha can be tuned automatically throughout the training process using meta-gradient methods. The method is evaluated on 6 OpenAI continuous control benchmarks (with delayed reward), and demonstrates better performance than several state-of-the-art prior works on intrinsic reward.

Overall, the paper is tackling an important challenge of reinforcement learning. Thus its potential impact is large. The paper is also well written and the results are promising. My main concern is that one important baseline is missing: [Pathak 2019]: "Self-supervised exploration via disagreement." This prior work is very relevant. It also used multiple models and the implementation was so much simpler than the proposed method in this paper. In addition, this paper also claims to be a generalization of [Pathak 2019]. Thus, adding [Pathak 2019] into the baselines for comparisons seems necessary.

I am also curious why not use MSE to measure the model-prediction error? The KL Divergence used in this paper introduces a lot of complexities, both in derivation and implementation (e.g. eq. 9, 10, 11). If MSE is not compatible with the proposed method, more discussions in the paper is welcome. Otherwise, adding an ablation to compare MSE with KLD would help, especially if KLD yields better results than MSE. This would justify the extra complexity introduced by using KLD.

The need of Condition 2 in Section 3.3 is not sufficiently motivated. How does this condition affect derivation, implementation and final results? It would be great to have a few sentences to clarity this.

In summary, this is a good paper. However, missing an important baseline [Pathak 2019] seems a significant oversight. I hope that this baseline will be added in the future version of this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper proposes an intrinsic reward formulation to address the challenge of sparse reward in reinforcement learning. The key idea is to learn multiple models. The prediction error of each model is used as a component of the intrinsic reward. These components are fused using an alpha-mean function. The parameter alpha can be tuned automatically throughout the training process using meta-gradient methods. The method is evaluated on 6 OpenAI continuous control benchmarks (with delayed reward), and demonstrates better performance than several state-of-the-art prior works on intrinsic reward.

The paper tackles an important challenge in reinforcement learning, and its potential impact is large. The paper is well written, and the results are promising. However, I have several concerns that need to be addressed.

Firstly, I find that one important baseline is missing: [Pathak 2019]: "Self-supervised exploration via disagreement." This prior work is very relevant, as it also uses multiple models to generate intrinsic rewards. The implementation of [Pathak 2019] is also relatively simple compared to the proposed method. Furthermore, the paper claims to be a generalization of [Pathak 2019]. Therefore, adding [Pathak 2019] to the baselines for comparisons seems necessary.

I am also curious about the choice of using KL Divergence to measure the model-prediction error. The KL Divergence introduces a lot of complexities, both in derivation and implementation (e.g., eq. 9, 10, 11). It would be helpful to discuss why MSE is not compatible with the proposed method or to add an ablation study comparing MSE with KLD. If KLD yields better results than MSE, this would justify the extra complexity introduced by using KLD.

The need for Condition 2 in Section 3.3 is not sufficiently motivated. It would be great to have a few sentences to clarify how this condition affects derivation, implementation, and final results.

In the proposed method, the fusion parameter α is learned adaptively over timesteps to maximize the performance. The ablation study shows that the general principle of RL is valid even in the adaptive fusion, and that a more aggressive strategy is needed in the early stage and a less aggressive strategy in the later stage. This observation is consistent with the general behavior of RL.

The proposed method uses a mixture of multiple prediction models to better approximate the unknown transition probability, and the intrinsic reward is generated by adaptive fusion learning with multiple prediction error values. The experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms existing intrinsic reward generation methods in the considered sparse environments.

To further improve the paper, it would be helpful to provide more discussions on the limitations of the proposed method and potential future work. Additionally, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs in more complex environments or with different types of intrinsic rewards.

In summary, this is a good paper. However, missing an important baseline [Pathak 2019] seems a significant oversight. I hope that this baseline will be added in the future version of this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors explore a model based intrinsic reward generation mechanism, in environment settings where the reward assignment is sparse. The authors used an ensemble of models,  and computed the alpha-mean value of their KL divergences with respect to the "true transitions". The alpha-mean serves as the intrinsic reward, with the parameter alpha being co-optimized during the training.  The authors demonstrated that their proposed approach yields top performance in six augmented MuJuCo continuous control tasks. 


Pros:

(1) A clearly written paper and easy to read and understand. 

(2) The approach demonstrated consistent top performance in all benchmarks.  

Cons:

(1) Although the author provides a few ablation studies. I propose to add at least one more study that compares the effectiveness of their method under different "sparsity" settings. In all experiments, the authors used a fixed interval of 40, at which extrinsic rewards are computed. I am interested to see if their conclusion can still hold when this interval is set at, for example, 10, 20, 60, etc.

(2) The alpha-mean method is one type of scale invariant transformation. I wonder if the authors have studied other transformation functions, or even scale invariant kernel functions?

(3) There are other sparse-reward tasks such as pushing/sliding/pick-and-place, which are under a different category than MuJuCo continuous control tasks. I am interested in seeing if the proposed approach can still perform in these settings. This will also help make the author's statements more convincing. 

Conclusion:
Please address my concerns raised in the "cons" section.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
In this paper, the authors explore a model-based intrinsic reward generation mechanism in environment settings where the reward assignment is sparse. The authors used an ensemble of models and computed the alpha-mean value of their KL divergences with respect to the "true transitions". The alpha-mean serves as the intrinsic reward, with the parameter alpha being co-optimized during the training. The authors demonstrated that their proposed approach yields top performance in six augmented MuJuCo continuous control tasks.

Pros:

(1) A clearly written paper and easy to read and understand.

(2) The approach demonstrated consistent top performance in all benchmarks.

Cons:

(1) Although the author provides a few ablation studies, I propose to add at least one more study that compares the effectiveness of their method under different "sparsity" settings. In all experiments, the authors used a fixed interval of 40, at which extrinsic rewards are computed. I am interested to see if their conclusion can still hold when this interval is set at, for example, 10, 20, 60, etc. The authors could evaluate their method on various sparsity settings, such as delaying extrinsic rewards for 10, 20, 40, 60, or even 80 timesteps, to demonstrate its robustness. This would provide more insight into how the proposed method handles different levels of sparsity.

(2) The alpha-mean method is one type of scale-invariant transformation. I wonder if the authors have studied other transformation functions, or even scale-invariant kernel functions? The authors could explore other scale-invariant transformations, such as the logarithmic or exponential functions, to compare their effectiveness with the alpha-mean method. Additionally, investigating the use of scale-invariant kernel functions, such as the radial basis function (RBF) kernel, could provide further insights into the proposed method.

(3) There are other sparse-reward tasks such as pushing/sliding/pick-and-place, which are under a different category than MuJuCo continuous control tasks. I am interested in seeing if the proposed approach can still perform in these settings. This will also help make the author's statements more convincing. The authors could evaluate their method on these tasks to demonstrate its generalizability and effectiveness in different domains.

Furthermore, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method compares to other intrinsic reward generation methods, such as curiosity-driven or surprise-based methods, in terms of performance and efficiency. The authors could also investigate the use of different exploration strategies, such as epsilon-greedy or entropy regularization, to see how they affect the performance of the proposed method.

In terms of the implementation, the authors used PPO as the base algorithm, which is a popular on-policy algorithm for continuous action control. However, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs with other algorithms, such as off-policy algorithms like Q-learning or SAC.

The authors also mentioned that the proposed method is useful not only for the specific problem considered but also for other problems involving numeric fusion with fusion learning. It would be interesting to see more discussion on this point and how the proposed method can be applied to other domains.

Conclusion:
Please address my concerns raised in the "cons" section.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors present a generalization of the model-prediction-error-based intrinsic reward method by fusing predictions from multiple models.
The authors considered the sparse reward scenario in reinforcement learning.

In related work, the authors mentioned that previous works on image spaces are not directly related to theirs. However, I did not understand what are the limitations or the caveats of the proposed method that leads to this conclusion.

Also in related work, I did not understand the purpose of detailing not related work in Sec. 2.2., I believe that the authors could use this space to discuss, for instance,
applications of fusion methods in reinforcement learning scenarios, such as: “Data fusion using Bayesian theory and reinforcement learning method”.
Another option, even better, is discussing approaches proposed in the line of investigation using ensembles
(e.g. “Model Ensemble-Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning”).

I would like to understand the computational costs involved in using such a fusion approach., both in terms of individual methods and alpha optimization.

In Fig. 2, it is hard to conclude that the gain over Module baseline is significant only considering the figure. I don’t know if the authors are considering some
statistical test when they mention significance, if this is the case, they should properly present the test and premisses.

In Sec.4.2.4, it is hard to see how the performance improves as K increases. First, only four values for K are a limitation to conclude this. 
Additionally, for instance, when considering the Walker2d dataset, the performance for K=2 is better than K=3.

References should point to the published work rather than the arxiv entries, when the former is available (e..g. Exploration by random network distillation, ICLR’19).

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
In this paper, the authors present a generalization of the model-prediction-error-based intrinsic reward method by fusing predictions from multiple models. The authors considered the sparse reward scenario in reinforcement learning.

The proposed method, which combines the predictions from multiple models using an adaptive fusion approach, appears to be a promising approach to tackle the challenge of sparse rewards in reinforcement learning. However, upon closer inspection, there are several limitations and caveats that need to be addressed.

The authors mention that previous works on image spaces are not directly related to theirs, but it would be beneficial to discuss the limitations or caveats of the proposed method that lead to this conclusion. For instance, the authors could elaborate on how the proposed method handles high-dimensional state spaces, which are common in image-based reinforcement learning tasks.

Furthermore, the related work section could be improved by discussing applications of fusion methods in reinforcement learning scenarios. For example, the authors could discuss how data fusion using Bayesian theory and reinforcement learning methods can be applied to their proposed approach. Another option would be to discuss approaches proposed in the line of investigation using ensembles, such as "Model Ensemble-Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning".

It would also be beneficial to understand the computational costs involved in using such a fusion approach, both in terms of individual methods and alpha optimization. The authors mention that the proposed method uses a meta-gradient optimization method to learn the fusion parameter alpha, but it would be helpful to provide more details on the computational complexity of this approach.

The performance comparison in Fig. 2 suggests that the proposed method outperforms the Module baseline, but it is hard to conclude that the gain is significant solely based on the figure. The authors mention that the gain is significant, but it would be more convincing if they provided statistical tests to support this claim. For instance, the authors could report the results of a paired t-test or a Wilcoxon signed-rank test to determine if the differences in performance are statistically significant.

In Sec. 4.2.4, the authors investigate the impact of the model order K on the performance of the proposed method. However, it is hard to see how the performance improves as K increases, given that only four values for K are considered. Additionally, for some datasets, such as Walker2d, the performance for K=2 is better than K=3, which suggests that the relationship between K and performance is not straightforward.

The references should point to the published work rather than the arxiv entries, when the former is available (e.g., Exploration by random network distillation, ICLR'19).

In conclusion, while the proposed method appears to be a promising approach to tackle the challenge of sparse rewards in reinforcement learning, there are several limitations and caveats that need to be addressed. The authors should provide more details on the computational complexity of the proposed method, discuss the limitations of the proposed method in handling high-dimensional state spaces, and provide statistical tests to support the claimed significant gains in performance.

Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

The decision remains the same, as the expanded review highlights the same concerns and limitations as the original review, leading to the same acceptance/rejection recommendation.

5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
The papers looks at the problem of using intrinsic rewards to help agent explore in sparse reward settings. The paper proposes combining multiple intrinsic rewards and proposes a meta-gradient based method to learning the fusion of these intrinsic rewards.


Strengths
1. Learning in a sparse rewards setting is a challenging and relevant problem to the community.
2. The paper is well written 
3. The idea of learning to use multiple intrinsic rewards and using different combinations of them at different times seems to be helpful in the experiments

Weaknesses/Comments/Questions
1. My main concern is that the contribution of the paper might not be sufficient. The main contribution claimed by the paper to me seems like the idea of combining different intrinsic rewards and also learning the hyper-parameter that controls the fusion. In practice different intrinsic rewards are indeed used together and varied at different times. Also learning RL related hyperparameters using meta-gradient (Meta-gradient RL) is not new.
2. I don't think Zheng et al is really meant for sparse reward settings. So not sure if that's the right SOTA baseline to compare with. Zheng et al is not expected to learn intrinsic rewards that guide initial exploration as it needs extrinsic feedback to learn these intrinsic rewards, else they are just random.
3. It would be good if the paper evaluates on some domains that are naturally sparse reward setting instead of converting a dense reward setting to a sparse reward setting by accumulating and delaying rewards.
4. If the main contribution of the paper is on how to fuse, then simple methods of fusing like learning a lenear combination or just a single layer neural network over the different model prediction errors to output the intrinsic reward to use all seem reasonable. Comparison with those would be good.
5. The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study to see if they indeed lead to the performance boost expected or if the performance comes from something else.
6. Clarification: Is it that all the experiments only fuse between two intrinsic rewards (prediction errors)? If the paper is about the importance of combining different prediction errors, it would be nice to have more than 2 and also some interestingly different model predictions and also analysis and comparison on what happens if we just use them separately. 

Questions to authors.
1. Please respond to the comments above. Thanks.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Step 1: Understand the Task
The task is to expand a peer review of a manuscript titled "ADAPTIVE MULTI-MODEL FUSION LEARNING FOR SPARSE-REWARD REINFORCEMENT LEARNING" while preserving all the core critique points from the original review.

## Step 2: Identify the Original Critique Points
The original review critiques the manuscript on several points:
1. The contribution of the paper might not be sufficient.
2. The comparison with Zheng et al. might not be fair since Zheng et al. is not meant for sparse reward settings.
3. It would be beneficial to evaluate the method on domains that are naturally sparse reward settings.
4. The comparison with simple methods of fusing like learning a linear combination or a single layer neural network would be good.
5. The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study.

## Step 3: Expand the Review
The manuscript proposes an adaptive multi-model fusion learning method for sparse-reward reinforcement learning, generalizing model-prediction-error-based intrinsic reward generation to multiple prediction models. The authors argue that using multiple models increases diversity in modeling error values and the chance to design a better intrinsic reward.

## Step 4: Detailed Critique and Suggestions
The paper addresses a challenging and relevant problem in reinforcement learning (RL) with sparse rewards. The writing is clear, and the idea of learning to use multiple intrinsic rewards and using different combinations of them at different times seems helpful in the experiments.

However, there are concerns about the contribution of the paper. The main contribution seems to be the idea of combining different intrinsic rewards and learning the hyper-parameter that controls the fusion. This is not entirely new, as different intrinsic rewards are used together and varied at different times in practice. Moreover, meta-gradient RL, used for learning the hyper-parameter, is not new.

The comparison with Zheng et al. might not be entirely fair since Zheng et al. uses a delayed reward environment, which differs from the sparse-reward environment based on thresholding. It would be beneficial to evaluate the proposed method on domains that are naturally sparse reward settings instead of converting a dense reward setting to a sparse reward setting by accumulating and delaying rewards.

The proposed adaptive fusion method is compared with several existing intrinsic reward generation methods, including single-model surprise, curiosity, hashing, information gain approximation, and the method using intrinsic reward module. The authors also compared their fusion method with the disagreement method using the variance of multiple predicted next states as the intrinsic reward.

## Step 5: Additional Critique Points
It would be interesting to see the performance of the proposed method when fused with more than two intrinsic rewards (prediction errors). The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study to see if they indeed lead to the performance boost expected or if the performance comes from something else.

## Step 6: Conclusion and Recommendation
The proposed method seems to outperform existing intrinsic reward generation methods in the considered sparse environments. However, the contribution of the paper might not be sufficient given the existing body of work in meta-gradient RL and intrinsic motivation.

The decision of "Marginally below acceptance threshold" (Rating: 5) remains unchanged.

The expanded review maintains the same tone and level of detail as the original, providing additional context and related observations.

## Step 7: Write Naturally
The expanded review is written naturally and fluently, integrating new content seamlessly.

The final answer is: 
Given the detailed instructions and the original review, here is the expanded review:

## Review

The paper "ADAPTIVE MULTI-MODEL FUSION LEARNING FOR SPARSE-REWARD REINFORCEMENT LEARNING" addresses a challenging problem in reinforcement learning with sparse rewards. The writing is clear, and the idea of learning to use multiple intrinsic rewards and using different combinations of them at different times seems helpful in the experiments.

However, there are concerns about the contribution of the paper. The main contribution seems to be the idea of combining different intrinsic rewards and learning the hyper-parameter that controls the fusion. This is not entirely new, as different intrinsic rewards are used together and varied at different times in practice. Moreover, meta-gradient RL, used for learning the hyper-parameter, is not new.

The comparison with Zheng et al. might not be entirely fair since Zheng et al. uses a delayed reward environment, which differs from the sparse-reward environment based on thresholding. It would be beneficial to evaluate the proposed method on domains that are naturally sparse reward settings instead of converting a dense reward setting to a sparse reward setting by accumulating and delaying rewards.

The proposed adaptive fusion method is compared with several existing intrinsic reward generation methods, including single-model surprise, curiosity, hashing, information gain approximation, and the method using intrinsic reward module. The authors also compared their fusion method with the disagreement method using the variance of multiple predicted next states as the intrinsic reward.

It would be interesting to see the performance of the proposed method when fused with more than two intrinsic rewards (prediction errors). The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study to see if they indeed lead to the performance boost expected or if the performance comes from something else.

The proposed method seems to outperform existing intrinsic reward generation methods in the considered sparse environments. However, the contribution of the paper might not be sufficient given the existing body of work in meta-gradient RL and intrinsic motivation.

The decision of "Marginally below acceptance threshold" (Rating: 5) remains unchanged.
