title: A Unified Framework for Convolution-based Graph Neural Networks

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain.
In particular, this framework allows to establish the relationships between standard, attention-based and topology-based GNNs.
Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based upon the proposed framework which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs, which achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although though to understand on first read.
I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature).
In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is build upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. Furthermore, the authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge.
Overall, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions.

Minor comments:

* It is not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization). Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework?

* A GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

============== Post Rebuttal Comments =================

I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. Sadly, I cannot find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript and therefore cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Hence, my rating will stay the same.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain. In particular, this framework allows establishing the relationships between standard, attention-based, and topology-based GNNs. Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based on the proposed framework, which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs and achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although it may require a second read to fully understand. I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature). In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

One of the significant strengths of this paper is its ability to unify various GNN models under a single framework. For instance, the authors provide a detailed analysis of how different GNN models, such as GCN, GAT, and PPNP, can be reformulated as particular instances of the optimal solution of the unified framework. This provides new insights into understanding graph learning modules from the graph signal perspective. The proposed framework can serve as a once-for-all platform for expert-designed modules on convolution-based approaches, where newly designed modules can be easily implemented on other networks as a plug-in module with trivial adaptations.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is built upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. The authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge. This would help to better understand the advantages and limitations of the proposed method.

Furthermore, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but it might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions. The authors validate the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer on several widely used semi-supervised node classification benchmarks, including transductive and inductive settings. However, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs on larger datasets or in more complex settings.

Regarding the minor comments, I would like to ask: Is it not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization)? Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework? Additionally, a GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

To further improve the paper, the authors could consider providing more detailed explanations of the proposed framework and its applications. For example, they could provide more examples of how the unified framework can be used to design new graph learning modules or how it can be applied to different domains.

In terms of the non-linearity analysis, I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. However, I could not find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript, and therefore, I cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Overall, the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of graph representation learning, and I believe that it deserves to be accepted.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper unifies several variants of the graph convolutional networks (GCNs) into a regularized quadratic optimization framework. Basically, the function to be optimized considers both to preserve node information and to perform graph Laplacian regularization, whose optimal solution gives a convolutional layer.

The unification is given by equations (3) and (18) and elaborated in section 3, which includes several methods including GCN, graph attentions, residual connections, concatenation, etc. This is not surprising: as a GCN layer (without activation) is a linear transformation, surely it is the optimum of a quadratic function. Broadly, any linear layer can be trivially formulated as a quadratic optimization problem. Still, I appreciate the authors' delicate work on unifying these diverse methods from an optimization perspective, which is useful and could lead to new methods.

From a technical perspective, the main novelty is that the authors further extend this framework by adding another feature variance term, so that the learned features have a certain variance. This is similar to the idea of batch normalization. This is reasonable because GCN tends to correlate the learned features with the graph Laplacian embedding (the optimal solution of the 2nd term in the authors' framework).

This is interesting but empirical. I would like to see how this additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph with some formal arguments. Unfortunately, this technic is mainly introduced as a heuristic and more detailed analysis is missing.

As in any regularization framework, there is an additional parameter involved that is the regularization strength (\alpha_3 in 21). Therefore the performance improvement is not surprising as the model is "enlarged". In the experiments or supplementary material, there should be a sensitivity study of this parameter.

On three citation graphs (that are commonly used to evaluate graph neural networks) and semi-supervised node classification tasks, the authors showed that the regularizer can bring marginal performance improvement.

Regarding Clarity, there are some typos in several places and rarely used phrases.

Overall, I don't feel excited after reading the article (although the contents are useful), as a large part of this work is on summarizing existing literature.  The "new bit" is mainly on the additional regularization term that is introduced as a heuristic.

Based on the novelty, a more proper venue for publishing this work could be relevant journals. Overall this submission presents a borderline case and I recommend weak acceptance.

As a minor comment: Equation (21) why not set \alpha_1=1?

----
After rebuttal:

Novelty: my assessment remains the same. It is not non-trivial enough to combine several linear operators into a unified optimization framework. Although the unification is useful, it is not a major novelty.

Thank you for the additional experiments on testing the hyper-parameter. As you mentioned instability, it is worth to have some toy example to demonstrate the instability and study the cause of such instability and show how to avoid such instability using the proposed regularizer. Clearly (19) is bounded. When \alpha_3 is large enough, the solution will be trivial.

Regarding non-linearity: the authors' framework is for unifying a graph convolution operator (that is one layer in a graph neural network). Nonlinear activation is another operator. This is not a major problem from my perspective.

Overall, I think this work has some value (although the novelty is not strong) and still recommend weak acceptance.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper presents a unified framework for convolution-based graph neural networks, which encompasses various existing methods, including GCN, graph attentions, residual connections, and concatenation. The unification is achieved through equations (3) and (18), which elaborate on the idea of formulating graph convolution operations as optimization problems in the graph Fourier space. Specifically, the authors propose a Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problem, which is shown to be equivalent to a graph convolution layer. This framework is useful and could lead to new methods, as it provides a general methodology for evaluating and relating different graph learning modules.

From a technical perspective, the main novelty of this work lies in the extension of this framework by adding a feature variance term, which is similar to the idea of batch normalization. This additional regularization term is designed to prevent the oversmoothing problem in graph convolutional networks, which tends to correlate the learned features with the graph Laplacian embedding. The authors provide a detailed analysis of this term and its effects on the learned features.

However, I would like to see more formal arguments on how this additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph. The authors mention that this technique is mainly introduced as a heuristic, and more detailed analysis is missing. It would be interesting to see how this regularization term affects the graph structure and the learned features.

As in any regularization framework, there is an additional parameter involved, which is the regularization strength (\alpha_3 in 21). Therefore, the performance improvement is not surprising, as the model is "enlarged". I appreciate the authors' efforts in providing a sensitivity study of this parameter in the experiments. However, I think it would be more convincing if the authors could provide more insights into the instability of the model when the regularization strength is large.

The authors conducted experiments on three citation graphs and semi-supervised node classification tasks, and showed that the regularizer can bring marginal performance improvement. However, I think the improvement is not significant enough to justify the novelty of this work. Moreover, I feel that a large part of this work is on summarizing existing literature, and the "new bit" is mainly on the additional regularization term that is introduced as a heuristic.

Regarding clarity, there are some typos in several places and rarely used phrases, which makes the paper hard to read.

Overall, I don't feel excited after reading the article, as I think the novelty is not strong enough. However, I do think that this work has some value, and the unified framework is useful for understanding and relating different graph learning modules. Therefore, I recommend weak acceptance.

As a minor comment, I think it would be more intuitive to set \alpha_1=1 in Equation (21).

## Expanded Review

The paper provides a unified graph convolution framework by establishing graph convolution operations with optimization problems in the graph Fourier domain. The authors show that most convolution-based graph neural networks can be derived from this framework by adding carefully designed regularizers. This provides new insights into understanding graph learning modules from the graph signal perspective.

The authors also propose a novel regularization technique under the framework to tackle the oversmoothing problem. The newly designed regularizer is shown to be effective in improving the generalization performance of graph learning models. However, I think it would be more convincing if the authors could provide more formal arguments on how this additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph.

The experiments conducted on three citation graphs and semi-supervised node classification tasks show that the regularizer can bring marginal performance improvement. However, I think the improvement is not significant enough to justify the novelty of this work. Moreover, I feel that a large part of this work is on summarizing existing literature, and the "new bit" is mainly on the additional regularization term that is introduced as a heuristic.

The authors mention that the proposed method can be implemented on various convolution-based approaches under the unified graph convolution framework. However, I think it would be more interesting to see how this method can be applied to other graph neural network architectures, such as GraphSAGE or JKNet.

The authors also mention that the proposed method can be used as a plug-in module for other convolution-based networks. However, I think it would be more convincing if the authors could provide more insights into how this method can be integrated with other graph neural network architectures.

## Additional Comments

* The authors could provide more insights into the instability of the model when the regularization strength is large.
* The authors could provide more formal arguments on how the additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph.
* The authors could provide more experiments on other graph neural network architectures, such as GraphSAGE or JKNet.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, this paper provides a unified graph convolution framework for understanding and relating different graph learning modules. The authors also propose a novel regularization technique under the framework to tackle the oversmoothing problem. However, I think the novelty of this work is not strong enough, and the improvement is not significant enough to justify the novelty of this work. Therefore, I recommend weak acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper presents a unified framework for graph convolutional neural networks based on regularized optimization, connecting different  variants of graph neural networks including vanilla, attention-based, and topology-based approaches. The authors also propose  a novel regularization technique to approach the oversmoothing problem in graph convolution. Experiments on the standard settings of node classification on Citeseer, Cora, and Pubmed prove the effectiveness of the proposed regularization techniques. 

Overall, this is a very interesting paper, proposing a unified framework for different variants of convolution-based graph neural networks. However, I also have a few concerns:

(1) The proposed framework is mainly designed for GNNs without considering the nonlinear transformation matrix. What if we have to consider the nonlinear transformation? Is the whole framework able to unify different GNNs?

(2) In the case of linear GNNs (without nonlinear transformation matrix), it is actually not surprising formulating GNNs as a regularized optimization problem. Such a regularization framework has already been discussed in the original GCN paper (Kipf et al. 2016). 

(3) In the case of linear GNNs, the overall framework is also very similar to the traditional label propagation framework (Zhou et al. Learning with Local and Global Consistency). Could you explain the difference?

(4) The new novel regularization technique seems to be similar to the one proposed in PairNorm (Zhao et al. 2020). Could you also explain the difference?

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

Overall, this is a very interesting paper, proposing a unified framework for different variants of convolution-based graph neural networks. However, I also have a few concerns:

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: The paper shows that several graph networks (GCN, attention GCN, PPNP, residual) can be unified under a common framework of Laplacian-regularised optimisation. Subsequently, different types of regularisation are combined to propose a new method for graph transduction, which is then empirically evaluated.

Significance: Laplacian regularisation is a classical approach for formulating/justifying graph transduction algorithms (multiple papers by Mikhail Belkin and Xiaojin Zhu around 2004-06). It is interesting to see that so many graph networks can also be unified in the same framework. A unified framework does aid in both theoretical analysis and implementation of GCNs.
However, the claims and derivation do not seem to account for the non-linear activation in the networks, and hence, significance of the work seems limited.

Quality: As noted above, non-linearity is not considered which makes the derivation significantly simpler. Moreover, the first-order approximation is quite misleading since even the proof do not seem to consider non-linear activation. 
Since the proposed method combines multiple types of regularisation, it is expected to perform better than other networks. However, it is not clear if the training time increases due to the complex regularisation.

Clarity and orginality: The paper is otherwise well written / organised, and the theoretical contributions (although technically straightforward) seem original and somewhat interesting.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Review:

**Summary**: The paper presents a unified framework for various graph neural networks (GNNs), including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), attention-based GCNs, and topology-based GCNs. The authors show that these models can be interpreted as Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problems with different carefully designed regularizers. This framework provides new insights into understanding the similarities and differences among various GNNs and may inspire new approaches for designing better models. As a showcase, the authors propose a novel regularization technique to tackle the oversmoothing problem in graph representation learning.

**Significance**: The proposed unified framework is significant as it provides a general methodology for evaluating and relating different graph learning modules. The Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problem offers a common ground for understanding various GNNs, which is essential for theoretical analysis and implementation. The authors' approach can help researchers and practitioners to better understand the strengths and weaknesses of different GNNs and design more effective models.

**Quality**: The paper is well-written and organized, and the theoretical contributions are technically sound. However, there are some limitations. The authors do not consider non-linear activations in the networks, which makes the derivation simpler but also limits the significance of the work. Additionally, the first-order approximation used in the paper may not accurately capture the behavior of the models.

**Clarity and Originality**: The paper is clear and well-organized, and the authors provide a thorough review of related work. The theoretical contributions are original and interesting, and the proposed framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field.

**Rating**: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

## Detailed Review

The paper proposes a unified framework for various GNNs, including GCNs, attention-based GCNs, and topology-based GCNs. The authors show that these models can be interpreted as Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problems with different carefully designed regularizers. This framework provides new insights into understanding the similarities and differences among various GNNs and may inspire new approaches for designing better models.

The authors provide a thorough review of related work and clearly explain the motivation and significance of their work. The proposed framework is technically sound, and the authors provide detailed derivations and proofs for their claims.

However, there are some limitations. The authors do not consider non-linear activations in the networks, which makes the derivation simpler but also limits the significance of the work. Additionally, the first-order approximation used in the paper may not accurately capture the behavior of the models.

The proposed framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field, and the authors provide a novel regularization technique to tackle the oversmoothing problem in graph representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

**Detailed Critique**

* The authors do not consider non-linear activations in the networks, which makes the derivation simpler but also limits the significance of the work.
* The first-order approximation used in the paper may not accurately capture the behavior of the models.
* The proposed framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field, but it is not clear if the training time increases due to the complex regularisation.

**Additional Comments**

* The paper is well-written and organized, and the authors provide a thorough review of related work.
* The proposed framework is technically sound, and the authors provide detailed derivations and proofs for their claims.
* The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

## Expanded Review

The paper presents a unified framework for various GNNs, including GCNs, attention-based GCNs, and topology-based GCNs. The authors show that these models can be interpreted as Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problems with different carefully designed regularizers. This framework provides new insights into understanding the similarities and differences among various GNNs and may inspire new approaches for designing better models.

The authors provide a thorough review of related work and clearly explain the motivation and significance of their work. The proposed framework is technically sound, and the authors provide detailed derivations and proofs for their claims.

One of the key contributions of the paper is the proposed unified framework, which provides a common ground for understanding various GNNs. The authors show that different GNNs can be reformulated as particular instances of the optimal solution of the Laplacian regularized least squares optimization problem. This framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field and provide new insights into understanding the strengths and weaknesses of different GNNs.

However, there are some limitations. The authors do not consider non-linear activations in the networks, which makes the derivation simpler but also limits the significance of the work. Additionally, the first-order approximation used in the paper may not accurately capture the behavior of the models.

The proposed framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field, and the authors provide a novel regularization technique to tackle the oversmoothing problem in graph representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

**Implications and Future Work**

The proposed unified framework has significant implications for the design of GNNs. By understanding the relationships between different GNNs, researchers and practitioners can design more effective models that leverage the strengths of different approaches.

One potential direction for future work is to extend the proposed framework to consider non-linear activations in the networks. This could involve developing new optimization problems that account for non-linearity.

Another potential direction is to explore the use of the proposed framework for other graph learning tasks, such as graph classification and graph generation.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, the paper presents a unified framework for various GNNs and provides new insights into understanding the similarities and differences among these models. The proposed framework has the potential to inspire new research in the field and provide new insights into understanding the strengths and weaknesses of different GNNs. However, there are some limitations, and future work is needed to extend the proposed framework to consider non-linear activations and explore its applications to other graph learning tasks.
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Summary: The paper shows that several graph networks (GCN, attention GCN, PPNP, residual) can be unified under a common framework of Laplacian-regularised optimisation. Subsequently, different types of regularisation are combined to propose a new method for graph transduction, which is then empirically evaluated.

Significance: Laplacian regularisation is a classical approach for formulating/justifying graph transduction algorithms (multiple papers by Mikhail Belkin and Xiaojin Zhu around 2004-06). It is interesting to see that so many graph networks can also be unified in the same framework. A unified framework does aid in both theoretical analysis and implementation of GCNs.
However, the claims and derivation do not seem to account for the non-linear activation in the networks, and hence, significance of the work seems limited.

Quality: As noted above, non-linearity is not considered which makes the derivation significantly simpler. Moreover, the first-order approximation is quite misleading since even the proof do not seem to consider non-linear activation. 
Since the proposed method combines multiple types of regularisation, it is expected to perform better than other networks. However, it is not clear if the training time increases due to the complex regularisation.

Clarity and orginality: The paper is otherwise well written / organised, and the theoretical contributions (although technically straightforward) seem original and somewhat interesting.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Practical Marginalized Importance Sampling with the Successor Representation

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
***Summary***
The paper proposes an approach to employ successor representation combined with marginalized importance sampling. The basic idea exploited in the paper consists of expressing the occupancies in terms of the successor representation and to model it via a linear combination of some features. This allows handling, although approximately, continuous state-action spaces. After having derived the objective function, an experimental evaluation on both Mujoco and Atari domains is presented, including an ablation study.

***Major***
- (About the linearity of the weight) Linear representations expressed in terms of a feature function are common in RL as the reward function can be often seen as a trade-off of different objectives encoded in the features. However, the choice of the linear representation in Equation (7) is based on the assumption that the marginalized weight is linear in the feature function. This assumption seems to me less justified compared to the one for the reward function. Clearly, a suitable feature design could overcome this limitation. Can the authors explain how the features \phi are selected or learned? 
- (Experimental evaluation) The results presented in the experimental evaluation are partially unsatisfactory, as also the authors acknowledge. It seems that there is no clear benefit in employing the marginalized importances sampling (both the baselines and the proposed approach) compared to standard deep temporal difference approaches. The authors suggest that this phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the quality of the marginalized weights is affected by the successor representation learned. I don't think this is the main weakness of the paper, but a reflection of the usefulness of the method in complex scenarios is necessary. Alternatively, it would be interesting to compare the proposed approach with DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (maybe toy ones) in which DualDICE and GradientDICE work well.


***Minor***
- The related work section should be moved later in the paper, maybe after Section 4
- Pag 2, two lines above Equation (2): the transition model is here employed as a distribution over the next state s' and the reward r, but the reward function is considered separately in the definition of MDP presented before
- Figures 2, 3, and 4: the plots are not readable when printing the paper in grayscale. I suggest using different linestyles and/or markers

***Typos***
- Pag 2: isn't -> is not
- Pag 2: doesn't -> does not
- Pag 8: the the -> the

***Overall***
The paper can be considered incremental compared to DualDICE. I did not find any fault, but I feel that the significance of contribution is currently insufficient for publication at ICLR. In particular, for a paper that proposes a practical variation of a theoretically sound algorithm, the experimental evaluation is essential. I think that the results are currently unable to clearly show the advantages of the proposed method.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
***Summary***
The paper proposes a practical approach to employ successor representation combined with marginalized importance sampling for off-policy evaluation. The basic idea exploited in the paper consists of expressing the occupancies in terms of the successor representation and modeling it via a linear combination of some features. This allows handling, although approximately, continuous state-action spaces. After having derived the objective function, an experimental evaluation on both Mujoco and Atari domains is presented, including an ablation study.

***Major***
- (About the linearity of the weight) Linear representations expressed in terms of a feature function are common in RL as the reward function can often be seen as a trade-off of different objectives encoded in the features. However, the choice of the linear representation in Equation (7) relies on the assumption that the marginalized weight is linear in the feature function. This assumption seems less justified compared to the one for the reward function. A suitable feature design could overcome this limitation. The authors mention that the features $\phi$ are selected based on prior work (Kulkarni et al., 2016; Machado et al., 2018a), but a more detailed explanation of the feature selection process would be beneficial. Specifically, it would be helpful to discuss how the features $\phi$ are learned or selected, and how they relate to the reward function.

- (Experimental evaluation) The results presented in the experimental evaluation are partially unsatisfactory, as also the authors acknowledge. It seems that there is no clear benefit in employing the marginalized importances sampling (both the baselines and the proposed approach) compared to standard deep temporal difference approaches. The authors suggest that this phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the quality of the marginalized weights is affected by the successor representation learned. However, a more in-depth analysis of the experimental results and a comparison with DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (e.g., toy problems) would provide more insight into the usefulness of the method in complex scenarios. For instance, the authors could provide a more detailed comparison of the performance of SR-DICE and the baseline methods on the toy random-walk experiment, and discuss the implications of the results.

***Minor***
- The related work section should be moved later in the paper, maybe after Section 4. This would improve the flow of the paper and allow the authors to focus on presenting their method before discussing related work.
- Pag 2, two lines above Equation (2): the transition model is here employed as a distribution over the next state $s'$ and the reward $r$, but the reward function is considered separately in the definition of MDP presented before. The authors may want to clarify this notation to avoid confusion.
- Figures 2, 3, and 4: the plots are not readable when printing the paper in grayscale. I suggest using different linestyles and/or markers to improve the readability of the plots.

***Typos***
- Pag 2: "isn't" -> "is not"
- Pag 2: "doesn't" -> "does not"
- Pag 8: "the the" -> "the"

***Overall***
The paper can be considered incremental compared to DualDICE. I did not find any fault, but I feel that the significance of contribution is currently insufficient for publication at ICLR. In particular, for a paper that proposes a practical variation of a theoretically sound algorithm, the experimental evaluation is essential. I think that the results are currently unable to clearly show the advantages of the proposed method.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper proposes SR-DICE, which uses a successor representation to compute DICE (discounted stationary distribution correction term).
* I am worried about both the technical and experimental qualities of this work. The theorems presented are either obvious or previously presented in other works. While the authors argue that the marginalized importance ratio is independent of the horizon (I assume that they are talking about the variance), MIS only alleviates the estimator variance's exponential dependence on the horizon to become the polynomial dependence on the horizon (as proved in Xie et al., Towards Optimal Off-Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning with Marginalized Importance Sampling, 2019). In the experiments, it is hard to believe that the GradientDICE and DualDICE perform that poorly having Log MSE larger than 0 while the GenDICE paper reports Log MSE less than -4 (HalfCheetah).
* The paper uses $\phi$ and $\psi$ learned by the previous deep successor representation learning algorithm, which is not meant to be used to learn marginal importance ratio. In particular, $\phi$ is learned by minimizing state, action, and reward reconstruction error and $\psi$ is the discounted sum of $\phi$. If we consider a case where $\pi$ only exploits a very small subset of state-action space, it is easy to see that the reconstruction error minimization in the dataset is not an optimal representation for the marginal importance ratio learning. In this sense, only the linear vector $w$ is used for the learning of marginal importance ratio.
* The experiment setting is not fair. Direct-SR and SR-DICE in their implementation have effectively 2 hidden layers, where DualDICE and GradientDICE in their implementation have a single hidden layer.
* The paper is hard to follow. Especially, notation abuse between the real reward and the virtual reward which is optimized to give a marginal importance ratio is very confusing (abuse between real Q and minimizer Q as well). Section 4.2 is also confusing because the authors imposes the problem of DualDICE that is not actually handled by SR-DICE.
* The idea of adopting successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio seems quite novel.
* Some people will be interested in this work, but I think the paper would not have much impact on the field.

Overall,

PROS:
* The idea of using successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio is novel.
* Avoids minimax formulation of other DICE algorithms, which makes the optimization very hard.

CONS:
* Not very meaningful theoretical results are presented, which mostly just confuse readers.
* Uses the representation that is not learned for marginal importance ratio learning
* Questionable experiment results


Minor details:
* y axis label is "Log MSE" for figures although the y axis is log scaled MSE.


--------------------------------------
Most of the concerns are addressed by the authors, and I raised my score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

The paper proposes SR-DICE, which uses a successor representation to compute DICE (discounted stationary distribution correction term). I am worried about both the technical and experimental qualities of this work. The theorems presented are either obvious or previously presented in other works. While the authors argue that the marginalized importance ratio is independent of the horizon (I assume that they are talking about the variance), MIS only alleviates the estimator variance's exponential dependence on the horizon to become the polynomial dependence on the horizon (as proved in Xie et al., Towards Optimal Off-Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning with Marginalized Importance Sampling, 2019). 

One major concern is that the paper uses $\phi$ and $\psi$ learned by the previous deep successor representation learning algorithm, which is not meant to be used to learn marginal importance ratio. In particular, $\phi$ is learned by minimizing state, action, and reward reconstruction error and $\psi$ is the discounted sum of $\phi$. If we consider a case where $\pi$ only exploits a very small subset of state-action space, it is easy to see that the reconstruction error minimization in the dataset is not an optimal representation for the marginal importance ratio learning. In this sense, only the linear vector $w$ is used for the learning of marginal importance ratio.

The experiment setting is not fair. Direct-SR and SR-DICE in their implementation have effectively 2 hidden layers, where DualDICE and GradientDICE in their implementation have a single hidden layer. The paper is hard to follow. Especially, notation abuse between the real reward and the virtual reward which is optimized to give a marginal importance ratio is very confusing (abuse between real Q and minimizer Q as well). Section 4.2 is also confusing because the authors impose the problem of DualDICE that is not actually handled by SR-DICE.

The idea of adopting successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio seems quite novel. Some people will be interested in this work, but I think the paper would not have much impact on the field.

## Detailed Critique

* The theoretical results presented in the paper are not very meaningful and mostly just confuse readers. The authors claim that the marginalized importance ratio is independent of the horizon, but this is not entirely accurate. MIS only alleviates the estimator variance's exponential dependence on the horizon to become the polynomial dependence on the horizon.
* The representation used for learning marginal importance ratio is not learned for this purpose. $\phi$ is learned by minimizing state, action, and reward reconstruction error and $\psi$ is the discounted sum of $\phi$. This representation may not be optimal for learning marginal importance ratio.
* The experiment setting is not fair. Direct-SR and SR-DICE have effectively 2 hidden layers, while DualDICE and GradientDICE have a single hidden layer.
* The notation used in the paper is confusing. The authors use $\phi$ and $\psi$ learned by the previous deep successor representation learning algorithm, but do not clearly explain how these representations are used for learning marginal importance ratio.

## Suggestions for Improvement

* Provide more detailed explanations of the theoretical results and how they relate to existing work.
* Use a more fair experiment setting, such as using the same architecture for all algorithms.
* Clarify the notation used in the paper and provide more explanations of how the representations are used for learning marginal importance ratio.
* Consider using a more suitable representation for learning marginal importance ratio.

## Conclusion

The paper proposes a novel idea of using successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio, but it has several concerns in terms of technical and experimental qualities. The paper needs to be revised to address these concerns and provide more detailed explanations of the theoretical results and experiment setting.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors propose SR-DICE based on deep SR for density ratio learning. Empirical advantages are observed in tested domains. Overall I think the idea is interesting and theoretically sound, but the experiments are not fully convincing.

It looks the main claim is that SR-DICE is better than other MIS methods because SR-DICE delegates the update propagation over the MDP to SR, while other MIS methods consider update propagation and density ratio learning together. To me this claim is not coupled with function approximation at all, so I would like to first see some experiments in the tabular setting. SR-DICE is a two-stage learning algorithm, i.e., SR learning + density ratio learning, both have hyperparameters to be tuned. GradientDICE and DualDICE are one-state learning algorithm. If in the tabular setting, we can empirically verify that under the best hyperparameter configuration of each algorithm (guaranteed by a thorough grid search), SR-DICE is more data efficient (counting the samples used in both stages) than GradientDICE and DualDICE, in terms of the density ratio prediction error, then the argument can be well backed. Well-controlled experiments like this, however, do not appear in the current submission.

Once deep networks are used for function approximation, we run into the problem of representation learning. The authors should at least include one more experiment, where MIS methods run directly on the pretrained deep SR features \psi_\pi(s) and/or \phi(s). In this way, we can distinguish whether the empirical advantage of SR-DICE comes from SR-DICE itself or the improved representation learning.

I'm also interested in seeing experiments for larger gammas, e.g., 0.999, 0.9999. I'm wondering if SR-DICE can consistently outperform GradientDICE with increasing discount factors.

Overall, I'm happy to increase the score if I have any misunderstanding or more convincing results are presented. I appreciate that the authors include deep TD and behavior R(\pi) as baselines. The empirical study has independent interest beyond SR-DICE. Moreover, deep TD is also referred to as Fitted-Q-Evaluation (FQE) in [x].

x: Voloshin, Cameron, et al. "Empirical Study of Off-Policy Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:1911.06854 (2019).

=======================

(Nov 24) The author response addressed my concerns and I therefore raised my score from 5 to 6. I particularly like the idea of using successor representation for density ratio learning.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

4 EXPERIMENTS

We benchmark the performance of SR-DICE on several high-dimensional domains in MuJoCo
(Todorov et al., 2012) and Atari (Bellemare et al., 2013), against several recent MIS methods. Our
results demonstrate two key ﬁndings regarding high-dimensional tasks.

SR-DICE signiﬁcantly outperforms the benchmark algorithms. We attribute this performance
gap to SR-DICE’s deep RL components, outperforming the baseline MIS methods in the same way that
deep RL outperforms traditional methods on high-dimensional domains. Unfortunately, part of this
performance gap is due to the fact that the baseline MIS methods scale poorly to challenging tasks.
In Atari we ﬁnd that the baseline MIS method exhibit unstable estimates, often reaching errors with
many orders of magnitude.

MIS underperforms deep RL. Although SR-DICE achieves a high performance, we ﬁnd its errors
are bounded by the quality of the SR. Consequently, we find that SR-DICE and the standard SR
achieve a similar performance across all tasks. Worse so, we find that using a deep TD method,
comparable to DQN (Mnih et al., 2015) for policy evaluation outperforms both methods. Although
the performance gap is minimal, for OPE there lacks a convincing argument for SR-DICE, or any
current MIS methods, which introduce unnecessary complexity. However, this does not mean MIS
is useless. We remark that the density ratios themselves are an independent objective which have
been used for applications such as policy regularization (Nachum et al., 2019b; Touati et al., 2019;
2020), imitation learning (Kostrikov et al., 2019), off-policy policy gradients (Imani et al., 2018; Liu
et al., 2019b; Zhang et al., 2019), and non-uniform sampling (Sinha et al., 2020). SR-DICE serves as a
stable, scalable approach for computing these ratios. We provide extensive experimental details in
the supplementary material and our code is made available.

The authors should consider providing more details on the implementation of SR-DICE. The
authors mention that they use a deep RL method comparable to DQN for policy evaluation. It
would be helpful to have more information on the architecture and hyperparameters used.

The authors should consider providing more analysis on the limitations of SR-DICE. Although SR-DICE
achieves a high performance on the benchmarks, it is bounded by the quality of the SR. This limits
the applicability of SR-DICE to tasks where the SR can be accurately learned. 

Here are the expanded peer review comments:

## Original Critique Points

The original review highlights several key points:

1. **Theoretical MIS methods are limited**: Current state-of-the-art MIS methods rely on complex optimization tricks and succeed mostly on simple toy problems.
2. **SR-DICE's performance**: SR-DICE significantly outperforms benchmark algorithms, likely due to its deep RL components.
3. **Comparison to deep RL**: SR-DICE's performance is comparable to that of standard SR and deep TD methods, such as DQN.
4. **Limitations of SR-DICE**: SR-DICE's performance is bounded by the quality of the SR, which can limit its applicability.

## Expanded Review

The manuscript presents a practical approach to Marginalized Importance Sampling (MIS) using the Successor Representation (SR). The authors propose SR-DICE, which leverages deep RL methodology to compute the density ratio between the state-action occupancy of a target policy and that of a sampling distribution.

One of the key contributions of SR-DICE is its ability to scale to high-dimensional domains. The authors demonstrate this by evaluating SR-DICE on a variety of challenging Atari and MuJoCo environments. The results show that SR-DICE significantly outperforms benchmark algorithms, likely due to its deep RL components. For instance, in the Atari environment, SR-DICE achieves a higher performance than the baseline MIS methods, which exhibit unstable estimates with errors of many orders of magnitude.

However, the authors also note that SR-DICE's performance is bounded by the quality of the SR. This limitation is significant, as it restricts the applicability of SR-DICE to tasks where the SR can be accurately learned. Furthermore, the authors find that SR-DICE's performance is comparable to that of standard SR and deep TD methods, such as DQN. This raises questions about the necessity of SR-DICE, as deep RL methods may be sufficient for OPE.

The authors should provide more details on the implementation of SR-DICE, including the architecture and hyperparameters used. This would help to facilitate reproducibility and provide a clearer understanding of the method's strengths and weaknesses. Additionally, the authors should consider providing more analysis on the limitations of SR-DICE. Although SR-DICE achieves a high performance on the benchmarks, its performance is bounded by the quality of the SR. This limits the applicability of SR-DICE to tasks where the SR can be accurately learned.

The manuscript also provides an interesting case study on DualDICE, a well-known MIS method. By viewing DualDICE through the lens of reward function optimization, the authors reveal its connection to residual algorithms and Bellman residual minimization. This provides valuable insights into the optimization properties and challenges of DualDICE.

In terms of theoretical contributions, the manuscript provides a thorough analysis of the SR-DICE method and its relationship to existing MIS methods. The authors derive the SR-DICE method by treating MIS as a reward function optimization problem, which leads to a more straightforward paradigm for direct reward function optimization. The manuscript also provides a detailed comparison of SR-DICE to existing MIS methods, highlighting its advantages and limitations.

The experimental evaluation of SR-DICE is comprehensive, with a thorough comparison to benchmark algorithms on several high-dimensional domains. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of SR-DICE in scaling to complex tasks, but also highlight its limitations.

Overall, the manuscript presents a practical and theoretically grounded approach to MIS using the SR. While SR-DICE shows promise, its limitations should be carefully considered, and further research is needed to fully understand its strengths and weaknesses.

## Additional Suggestions

* The authors should consider providing more intuition on the behavior of SR-DICE in different environments. For instance, how does SR-DICE perform in tasks with varying levels of complexity or uncertainty?
* The manuscript could benefit from a more detailed analysis of the computational complexity of SR-DICE. How does SR-DICE compare to existing MIS methods in terms of computational efficiency?
* The authors should consider providing more discussion on the implications of their results for real-world applications. For instance, how could SR-DICE be used in practice, and what are the potential benefits and limitations of its use?

x: Voloshin, Cameron, et al. "Empirical Study of Off-Policy Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:1911.06854 (2019).

=======================

(Nov 24) The author response addressed my concerns and I therefore raised my score from 5 to 6. I particularly like the idea of using successor representation for density ratio learning.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Strengths:

1) The experiments are extensive, and clearly demonstrate the merits as compared to prior benchmarks for off policy RL.

2) The contextual discussion is clear, well-motivates the proposed approach, and gives a nice overview of how importance sampling and off policy RL intersect.



Weaknesses:


1) Theorem 1 seems vacuous. The proof is a simple exercise in elementary calculus -- one may easily show the minimizer of a quadratic is the least squares estimator. The authors need to better explain what is the technical novelty of this statement, and why it is meaningful. Upon inspection it does not seem to qualify as a theorem. This is also true of Theorems 2 and 3. Therefore, I feel the conceptual contribution is not enough to warrant acceptance.

2) The notion of successor representation seems identical to the occupancy measure, which in order to estimate, requires density estimation, which is extremely sample inefficient. Can the authors comment about how to estimate the successor representation efficiently? There is very little discussion of sample complexity throughout, which is somewhat alarming because a key selling point of off-policy schemes for RL is that they alleviate the need for sampling from the MDP transition dynamics.

3) The actual algorithm pseudo-code is missing from the body of the paper, which is permissible because it is in the appendix. However, the structural details of how the algorithm works iteratively and how it departs from previous works are also not explained. That is, while derivation details are presented, iterative details are not. In my opinion this should be strictly required in the body of the paper, as well as contextual discussion of what is similar/different from previous works, but all I could find was high level presentation of objectives minimized at various sections, but not how they are interlinked.

4) The background discussion is disjointed. There is a preliminaries section on page 5, as well as a background section 3.


Minor Comments:


1) References missing related to the sample/parameterization complexity issues associated with importance sampling: 

Koppel, A., Bedi, A. S., Elvira, V., & Sadler, B. M. (2019). Approximate shannon sampling in importance sampling: Nearly consistent finite particle estimates. arXiv preprint arXiv:1909.10279.

Strengths:

1. The experiments are extensive and clearly demonstrate the merits of the proposed approach compared to prior benchmarks for off-policy RL. The authors have evaluated their method, SR-DICE, on a variety of challenging Atari and MuJoCo environments, showcasing its performance and stability.

2. The contextual discussion is clear and well-motivates the proposed approach. The authors provide a nice overview of how importance sampling and off-policy RL intersect, highlighting the benefits of Marginalized Importance Sampling (MIS) methods.

Weaknesses:

1. Theorem 1 seems vacuous, as the proof is a simple exercise in elementary calculus. The authors need to better explain the technical novelty of this statement and why it is meaningful. Specifically, the authors should clarify how this theorem contributes to the field of off-policy evaluation and what implications it has for the proposed algorithm.

2. The notion of successor representation seems identical to the occupancy measure, which, in order to estimate, requires density estimation. This is extremely sample inefficient. The authors should comment on how to estimate the successor representation efficiently, especially in high-dimensional spaces. There is very little discussion of sample complexity throughout, which is somewhat alarming because a key selling point of off-policy schemes for RL is that they alleviate the need for sampling from the MDP transition dynamics.

3. The actual algorithm pseudo-code is missing from the body of the paper, which is permissible because it is in the appendix. However, the structural details of how the algorithm works iteratively and how it departs from previous works are also not explained. That is, while derivation details are presented, iterative details are not. In my opinion, this should be strictly required in the body of the paper, as well as contextual discussion of what is similar/different from previous works.

4. The background discussion is disjointed. There is a preliminaries section on page 5, as well as a background section 3. The authors should integrate these sections and provide a more cohesive overview of the relevant background material.

Minor Comments:

1. References are missing related to the sample/parameterization complexity issues associated with importance sampling. Specifically, the authors may want to consider citing Koppel et al. (2019), which discusses approximate Shannon sampling in importance sampling.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

**Expanded Justification**

The authors have made significant contributions to the field of off-policy RL with their proposed algorithm, SR-DICE. However, there are several areas that require further clarification and improvement.

One of the primary concerns is the lack of technical novelty in Theorem 1. The authors should provide more context on why this theorem is meaningful and how it contributes to the field. Additionally, they should discuss the implications of this theorem for the proposed algorithm and its performance.

Another area of concern is the efficiency of estimating the successor representation. The authors should provide more details on how to estimate the successor representation efficiently, especially in high-dimensional spaces. This is crucial because the sample complexity of the algorithm is a critical factor in its overall performance.

The authors should also provide more details on the algorithm's iterative process and how it departs from previous works. This will help to contextualize the proposed algorithm and provide a clearer understanding of its strengths and weaknesses.

Finally, the authors should integrate the preliminaries and background sections to provide a more cohesive overview of the relevant background material. This will help to improve the overall flow of the paper and make it easier to follow.

Overall, while the authors have made significant contributions to the field of off-policy RL, there are several areas that require further clarification and improvement. With revisions to address these concerns, the paper could make a stronger contribution to the field.

title: Exploring Zero-Shot Emergent Communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors study the zero-shot emergent non-verbal communication in this paper. Different from most papers on emergent communication. this paper uses the motion of three-joint agents. The agents meet partners that they have never seen in the training phase, presenting the challenge of the universal protocol. To make a universal protocol possible, the authors study intents sampled from Zipf distribution and energy regulation. The authors conducted experiments on tasks with 2, 5, 10 intents. The results show that providing latent energy feature is essential for zero-shot coordination. To achieve better than chance accuracy on tasks with 10 intents, a torque curriculum is needed. 

Pros:
1. The setting of ZS coordination with non-verbal emergent communication is novel and interesting. 
2. The use of energy regulation and Zipf distribution is natural and intuitive. The results also support the intuition. 

Cons:
What is the aim of using motion as a communication protocol? In human communication, actions are often used as an iconic gesture to describe intents that are not well described by words. In this sense, humans can perform zero-shot non-verbal emergent communication since the iconic gestures are always universal. In other words, a grounded world should be used to study ZS coordination. This paper seems to study a problem that is not realistic.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The authors study the zero-shot emergent non-verbal communication in this paper, which is an interesting and novel setting. Different from most papers on emergent communication, this paper uses the motion of three-joint agents. The agents meet partners that they have never seen in the training phase, presenting the challenge of the universal protocol. To make a universal protocol possible, the authors study intents sampled from Zipf distribution and energy regulation. The authors conducted experiments on tasks with 2, 5, 10 intents. The results show that providing latent energy feature is essential for zero-shot coordination. To achieve better than chance accuracy on tasks with 10 intents, a torque curriculum is needed.

One of the strengths of this paper is its exploration of a novel setting for zero-shot emergent communication, using embodied agents in a 3D environment. The use of energy regulation and Zipf distribution is natural and intuitive, and the results support the intuition that these elements can facilitate zero-shot coordination. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the challenges associated with optimizing in a continuous space for a monotonic ordering on energy values, and their proposed solutions, including inducing implicit latent structure through physical energy exertion and providing explicit latent structure, are well-motivated and interesting.

However, there are some concerns regarding the realism of the setting. The authors mention that the use of motion as a communication protocol is motivated by human communication, where actions are often used as iconic gestures to describe intents that are not well-described by words. However, in human communication, these gestures are often grounded in a shared understanding of the world and are not solely based on the motion itself. In this sense, the paper seems to study a problem that is not entirely realistic, as it lacks a grounded world to study ZS coordination.

Furthermore, while the authors provide a detailed analysis of the optimization challenges and propose solutions, it is not entirely clear whether these solutions would scale to more complex tasks or larger numbers of intents. The results show that even with the proposed solutions, zero-shot coordination performance is still limited, especially for tasks with 10 intents. This suggests that further research is needed to develop more robust methods for learning generalizable communication protocols.

In terms of the technical contributions, the paper proposes an interesting approach to inducing implicit latent structure through physical energy exertion and provides a thorough analysis of its effectiveness. The use of a torque curriculum to improve communication generalization is also an interesting idea. However, it would be helpful to provide more intuition on why this curriculum is effective and how it relates to the underlying optimization problem.

Overall, I think the paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, with a rating of 6. While it presents an interesting and novel setting, and makes some important contributions, there are still some concerns regarding the realism of the setting and the scalability of the proposed solutions.

##########################################################################

Summary:

The paper deals with agents that communicate non-verbally via actuating their joints in a 3D environment. The authors show that the agents should be able to learn protocols that can generalize to novel partners. Furthermore, the authors find that the current training approaches are brittle, and they propose and evaluate approaches to address this challenge.

##########################################################################

Overall, I vote for accepting. The authors develop and evaluate an algorithm that allows for emergent communication which generalizes to novel partners. Furthermore, the authors openly communicate and address the brittleness of the training.

##########################################################################

* Novel algorithm to perform emergent communication via joint actuations
* Algorithm allows for generalization to novel partners
* Suggestions to improve training stability

##########################################################################

Cons:

* It would be desirable to train the agents N times with different initializations and report mean and std of the performance
* More experiments and evaluation results with various joint numbers, intents, potentially different communication strategies would be helpful 

##########################################################################

Misc:

The text in Figure 1 is too small.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper explores the concept of emergent communication in multi-agent systems, where agents learn to communicate non-verbally by actuating their joints in a 3D environment. The authors demonstrate that under mild assumptions, agents can learn protocols that generalize to novel partners. However, they also find that current training approaches are brittle and propose methods to address this challenge.

## Reasons

Overall, I vote for accepting this paper. The authors develop and evaluate an algorithm that enables emergent communication, which generalizes to novel partners. They also openly discuss and address the brittleness of the training process.

## Pros

* The paper presents a novel algorithm for emergent communication via joint actuations, allowing for generalization to novel partners.
* The algorithm's ability to generalize to new partners is a significant contribution, as it demonstrates the potential for agents to learn communication protocols that are not specific to a particular partner.
* The authors provide suggestions to improve training stability, which is a crucial aspect of emergent communication.

## Cons

* One potential improvement could be to train the agents multiple times with different initializations and report the mean and standard deviation of the performance. This would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the algorithm's robustness.
* Additional experiments and evaluation results with varying joint numbers, intents, and potentially different communication strategies would be helpful in further validating the algorithm's effectiveness.

## Detailed Review

The paper presents a thorough exploration of emergent communication in embodied multi-agent populations. The authors' approach to inducing latent structure through physical energy exertion is well-motivated and effectively implemented. The use of a Zipf distribution over intents and an energy-based curriculum are key components of the algorithm, and their impact on zero-shot coordination is carefully evaluated.

The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm can achieve high zero-shot coordination success rates, particularly when the external observer is provided with explicit latent structure. The authors also provide valuable insights into the challenges of optimizing energy values and the importance of pretraining actors on energy.

The visualization of learned behaviors and energy exertions provides a useful illustration of the algorithm's effectiveness. The authors' discussion of the limitations of current approaches and potential avenues for future work is also appreciated.

## Minor Comments

* The text in Figure 1 is too small and may be difficult to read.
* The authors may want to consider providing additional details about the experimental setup, such as the specific joint numbers and intents used.

## Rating

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

The expanded review preserves the original critique points, decision, and tone while providing additional context and observations. The rating remains unchanged, reflecting the paper's marginal but significant contribution to the field of emergent communication.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper studies how gesture-based (non-verbal) communication can emerge in embodied multi-agent population.
In their problem setting, there is a set of agents, and an observer. Given an intent sampled from a Zipf distribution, the agents generate energy-efficient trajectories, and the observer predicts the intent given these trajectories. Zero-shot coordination is enabled by combining energy cost with Zipf distribution (which is monotonic), since energy cost is grounded in the environment. The authors evaluate zero-shot coordination performance by evaluating a third-party observer trained and tested on two separate subsets of agents from the population respectively.

Pros: 
1. The idea of using energy cost and combining energy cost with a non-uniform distribution for emergent communication is new.
2. The idea of exploiting common-knowledge grounded in the environment for zero-shot coordination is new and interesting. 

Cons:
1. The paper is sometimes hard to follow with missing details.
2. The authors failed to discuss options other than energy cost for ZS coordination, and why energy cost is preferred compared with other options.
3. The assumption of a monotonic ordering for intent distribution is strong. The assumption that energy cost is available during training/testing is also strong.

I like the idea of exploiting a universal (common-knowledge) cost grounded in the environment for zero-shot coordination.
However, is energy cost the only option here? what about trajectory length (spatial), cumulative angular velocity change, etc. I understand energy cost may be a better option, but the authors should discuss and compare with other options. Meanwhile, in many multi-agent environment, the only available inputs are visual inputs and energy cost may not available. Finally, even if the learned policy achieved zero-shot coordination with a third-party observer, it is not human interpretable since humans cannot directly observe energy cost. Any thoughts on learneing human-interpretable gesture-based language?

While Figure 1 is "Overview of Learning System", it does not really helps the readers to get the key idea of the paper. It is better to have a more detailed figures explaining your problem setting and motivation.

The assumption that the intent is from a monotonic distribution seems a little bit strong to me. It is better to have experiments with other distributions (not uniform). e.g. a subset of intents has equal probabilities to be selected.

In Section 3 Problem Setting, you mentioned that "After T steps, the episode terminates, and the sequence of ...". So the number of steps per trajectory is fixed? Why?

In 5.1 where are the "unseen observer agents in the population" come from? My understanding is that there is one observer and a set of agents during the training. But here it implies that there are more than one observer during the training?

Algorithm 1 in A.2 is not clear. For example, Line 2 says "Let A<-population of agents". However, "A" never showed up again in the remaining of the algorithm; The term discriminator (line 3), receiver (line 5), and observer (line 15) are used interchangeably in Algorithm 1.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
